question:
Three distinct points with integer coordinates lie in the plane on a
circle of radius $r>0$.  Show that two of these points are separated by a
distance of at least $r^{1/3}$.
solution:
Let $a,b,c$ be the distances between the points. Then the area of the triangle
with the three points as vertices
is $abc/4r$. On the other hand, the area of a triangle whose vertices
have integer coordinates is at least 1/2 (for example,
by Pick's Theorem). Thus $abc/4r \geq 1/2$,
and so
\[
\max\{a,b,c\} \geq (abc)^{1/3} \geq (2r)^{1/3} > r^{1/3}.
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Three distinct points with integer coordinates lie in the plane on a
circle of radius $radiusv>0$.  Show that two of these points are separated by a
distance of at least $radiusv^{1/3}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Let $distone,disttwo,distthree$ be the distances between the points. Then the area of the triangle
with the three points as vertices
is $distone disttwo distthree/4 radiusv$. On the other hand, the area of a triangle whose vertices
have integer coordinates is at least 1/2 (for example,
by Pick's Theorem). Thus $distone disttwo distthree/4 radiusv \geq 1/2$,
and so
\[
\max\{distone,disttwo,distthree\} \geq (distone disttwo distthree)^{1/3} \geq (2 radiusv)^{1/3} > radiusv^{1/3}.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Three distinct points with integer coordinates lie in the plane on a
circle of radius $lampstand>0$.  Show that two of these points are separated by a
distance of at least $lampstand^{1/3}$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Let $raincoat,theorems,sunlight$ be the distances between the points. Then the area of the triangle
with the three points as vertices
is $raincoat\,theorems\,sunlight/4lampstand$. On the other hand, the area of a triangle whose vertices
have integer coordinates is at least 1/2 (for example,
by Pick's Theorem). Thus $raincoat\,theorems\,sunlight/4lampstand \geq 1/2$,
and so
\[
\max\{raincoat,theorems,sunlight\} \geq (raincoat\,theorems\,sunlight)^{1/3} \geq (2lampstand)^{1/3} > lampstand^{1/3}.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Three distinct points with integer coordinates lie in the plane on a
circle of radius $centroid>0$.  Show that two of these points are separated by a
distance of at least $centroid^{1/3}$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Let $proximity,closeness,adjacency$ be the distances between the points. Then the area of the triangle
with the three points as vertices
is $proximity closeness adjacency/4centroid$. On the other hand, the area of a triangle whose vertices
have integer coordinates is at least 1/2 (for example,
by Pick's Theorem). Thus $proximity closeness adjacency/4centroid \geq 1/2$,
and so
\[
\max\{proximity,closeness,adjacency\} \geq (proximity closeness adjacency)^{1/3} \geq (2centroid)^{1/3} > centroid^{1/3}.
\]

garbled_string.question:
Three distinct points with integer coordinates lie in the plane on a
circle of radius $mxtkjesu>0$.  Show that two of these points are separated by a
distance of at least $mxtkjesu^{1/3}$.
garbled_string.solution:
Let $qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,prbqlvex$ be the distances between the points. Then the area of the triangle
with the three points as vertices
is $qzxwvtnphjgrkslaprbqlvex/4mxtkjesu$. On the other hand, the area of a triangle whose vertices
have integer coordinates is at least 1/2 (for example,
by Pick's Theorem). Thus $qzxwvtnphjgrkslaprbqlvex/4mxtkjesu \geq 1/2$,
and so
\[
\max\{qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla,prbqlvex\} \geq (qzxwvtnphjgrkslaprbqlvex)^{1/3} \geq (2mxtkjesu)^{1/3} > mxtkjesu^{1/3}.
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $r>0$.  Five distinct points whose 
coordinates are all even integers lie on a common circle of radius $r$ in the plane.  Prove that among these five points there exist two whose distance is at least $2\,r^{1/3}$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Label any three of the given points and denote the pairwise distances between them by a, b, c.  These three points are vertices of a triangle inscribed in the circle of radius r, so

    Area = abc/(4r).

Next we estimate this area from below.  Because every coordinate of every vertex is an even integer, each vertex can be written as 2(x,y) with x,y\in \mathbb{Z}.  Dividing all coordinates by 2 therefore maps our triangle bijectively onto a triangle whose vertices have ordinary integer coordinates.  The linear map (x,y)\mapsto (2x,2y) multiplies areas by the factor 2^2=4, hence

    Area of original triangle = 4\times (Area of image triangle).

For a triangle with integer-coordinate vertices the classical Pick (or Pick-type) argument gives a minimal positive area of 1/2.  Consequently, the original triangle has area at least

    4\times (1/2) = 2.

Combining these two facts yields

    abc/(4r) \geq  2  \Rightarrow   abc \geq  8r.

Among the three numbers a,b,c let d = max{a,b,c}.  Then d^3 \geq  abc, so from abc \geq  8r

    d^3 \geq  8r  \Rightarrow   d \geq  (8r)^(1/3) = 2 r^(1/3).

Thus the chosen triple already contains two points at distance at least 2 r^(1/3).  A fortiori, the original collection of five points must contain such a pair as well.  This establishes the desired bound.

core_steps: Form the triangle determined by three lattice points on the circle.
Express its area with sides a,b,c and circumradius r:  A = abc / (4r).
Invoke lattice-area quantization (Pick): any non-degenerate lattice triangle has area ≥ A₀ (here 1/2).

slot_number_of_points.description: Total lattice points given on the circle (only three are needed for the argument).

slot_min_lattice_area.description: Smallest positive area attainable by a lattice triangle, entering the Pick bound.

slot_distance_threshold_factor.description: Leading constant multiplying r^{1/3} in the claimed separation; any value ≤ (4·(min lattice area))^{1/3} would still be guaranteed.
